title: The Seven Personas: Room by Room
paragraphs: Every person you approach will default to one of these. Some shift between them. But in the moment of confrontation, one will dominate. Recognise it. Adjust. And move through the house accordingly.
1. The Wall — "I'm Done Talking."
Underneath: Overwhelmed. Nervous system flooded. Heart rate past the threshold for rational engagement. The Wall is not punishing you. The Wall is drowning.

The Knock: Don't knock at all. Slide a note under the door. Hours of advance notice. Maybe a day. "There's something I'd like to talk about. Not now. Could we sit down tomorrow after dinner?" Let their system regulate before you even approach.

The Foyer: Short and warm. "Thanks for being willing to do this. I know this isn't easy. Twenty minutes. If it gets too much, we take a break." You're giving them an exit ramp. That's what makes them stay.

The Kitchen: Lead with your stuff. Don't start with what they did. Start with how you've been feeling. "I've noticed I've been pulling away too. I don't want that." When you go first, you lower their defences.
The Lounge Room: One issue. One conversation. No ambush. The Wall can handle one thing. Not five. Pick the most important and stay there.
Psalm 46:10 — "Be still, and know that I am God."
Stillness is not silence. Teach the Wall the difference.
2. The Deflector — "You're Making This a Bigger Deal Than It Is."
Underneath: Terrified. If they let you land on the real issue, they have to face something about themselves they're not ready to see.
The Knock: Once. Gently. Specific. "I want to talk about what happened Tuesday night." Specificity removes escape routes.
The Foyer: Let them deflect once. Then name it. "I hear you. But that's not what I'm here about. I'm here about this one thing." Warmth, not frustration.
The Kitchen: Shrink the ask. "I'm not asking you to solve everything. Just hear one thing." Make it small enough they can't justify running.
The Lounge Room: Hold the line without aggression. Every redirect, bring it back gently. Patience is your currency. They'll stop running if you stop chasing.
Galatians 6:1 — "Brothers and sisters, if someone is caught in a sin, you who live by the Spirit should restore that person gently. But watch yourselves, or you also may be tempted."
Gently. Not weakly. There's steel inside gentleness. The Deflector needs to feel it.
3. The Volcano — "WHAT ABOUT YOU?"
Underneath: Wounded. The eruption is protection. If they get loud enough, you'll stop talking.
The Knock: Soft. Stand back. "I'm not here to fight. I'm here because I care." Signal from the porch that this is not an attack.
The Foyer: Let the first eruption pass. Don't match it. Don't escalate. Don't retreat. Stand still. The first eruption is a test. Stay calm, they calm down.
The Kitchen: Validate the emotion underneath the volume. "I can hear this is hitting something deep. I want to understand what's underneath the frustration." Respond to the wound, not the noise.

The Lounge Room: Circle back when it's quiet. The real conversation happens twenty minutes after the eruption, when the smoke clears. The Volcano respects people who don't run and don't escalate. Be the steady ground.
Proverbs 15:18 — "A hot-tempered person stirs up conflict, but the one who is patient calms a quarrel."
4. The Minimiser — "It's Really Not That Serious."
Underneath: Denial. They believe if they make the problem small enough, it disappears. It won't.
The Knock: Evidence, not emotion. "This has happened three times in two weeks. I want to talk about the pattern." Facts are harder to shrink.
The Foyer: Validate their view, then hold yours. "Maybe it doesn't seem big from where you sit. But it's affecting me. I need you to hear that."
The Kitchen: Questions, not statements. "If this keeps happening, where do you think we'll be in a year?" Let them connect the dots. They won't accept your diagnosis. They might accept their own.
The Lounge Room: Don't accept the shrink. "Okay fine, I hear you" is not the lounge room. It's the foyer wearing a different shirt. "I appreciate that. Now what does different actually look like?"
Hebrews 12:15 — "See to it that no one falls short of the grace of God and that no bitter root grows up to cause trouble and defile many."
Buried things don't die. They grow roots. The Minimiser needs to understand that.
5. The Performer — "You're Right. I'll Do Better."
Underneath: Exhausted. The performance ends the conversation, not the problem. Beautiful apology. Nothing changes.
The Knock: They'll open immediately. The danger isn't getting in. It's believing you've arrived in the lounge room when you're still in the foyer.
The Foyer: Acknowledge the apology. Don't accept it as the finish line. "I've heard these words before. Help me believe them this time."
The Kitchen: Feelings to specifics. "What does 'better' look like? What will I see next week?" The Performer can't perform specifics. Specifics need follow-through.
The Lounge Room: Build in accountability. "Can we check in on this in two weeks?" The lounge room isn't the apology. It's the plan.
James 2:17 — "In the same way, faith by itself, if it is not accompanied by action, is dead."
Words without action are a performance. Performances end when the lights go down.
6. The Prosecutor — "Let Me Tell You What YOU Did."
Underneath: Identity protection. If they prosecute you, they don't face the verdict on themselves. Classic DARVO: Deny, Attack, Reverse Victim and Offender.
The Knock: Olive branch visible. "I'm not perfect either. I'm coming as someone who wants us to be better." Disarm the counter-attack before it launches.
The Foyer: Expect the cross-examination. Don't bite. "You're right, I've done things wrong too. We can talk about those. But not now. Now we stay on this one thing."
The Kitchen: Separate issue from identity. "I'm not saying you're a bad person. I'm saying this one thing is hurting us." Character off the table. Behaviour on it.
The Lounge Room: Promise the other conversation. "You'll get your turn. I promise." They need to know they'll be heard. That's what gets them to sit down.

Romans 2:1 — "You, therefore, have no excuse, you who pass judgment on someone else, for at whatever point you judge another, you are condemning yourself, because you who pass judgment do the same things."
7. The Ghost — "…"
Underneath: Grieving. Already leaving. The Ghost doesn't fight. Doesn't deflect. Doesn't erupt. They just… disappear.
The Knock: Don't knock. Show up. Face to face. "I've noticed you pulling back. I'm not willing to let this go quietly." They need to know their absence was noticed.
The Foyer: Don't ask why. Ask what. "What do you need from me right now?" "Why" is interrogation. "What" is invitation.
The Kitchen: Presence without pressure. Sit with them. Be in the room. Don't fill the silence. Sometimes the kitchen with the Ghost is just being there. Quietly. Faithfully.
The Lounge Room: Multiple visits. They don't open up first time. Or second. But every time you show up: "You matter. I noticed. I'm not giving up." Earned over weeks, not one sitting.
Luke 15:4 — "Suppose one of you has a hundred sheep and loses one of them. Doesn't he leave the ninety-nine in the open country and go after the lost one until he finds it?"
Jesus didn't text the lost sheep. He left everything and went after it. That's the posture the Ghost needs to see.
